<defs>

<h2 class="river-title river-title--author">The Latest from Kirsten Korosec</h2>

	<header class="post-block__header">

{"ads":{"gemini_enabled":false,"gemini_codes":{"position_4":{"mobile":"ae64471c-14c2-4457-8e9b-a5190206eafd","desktop":"b2018d9e-dedc-4e85-8823-b06917beb892"},"position_8":{"mobile":"619392a1-b497-4aa9-b683-24aab7246c16","desktop":"05c0b21d-e742-494d-ac59-474e3feaa4b8"}}},"assetURI":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/themes\/techcrunch-2017","breaking":"0","live_event":"0","livestream":"","feature_islands":{"homepage":[{"id":2636845,"date":"2023-12-01T14:43:04","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T22:43:04","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636845"},"modified":"2023-12-01T15:19:36","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T23:19:36","slug":"23andme-says-hackers-accessed-significant-number-of-files-about-users-ancestry","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/23andme-says-hackers-accessed-significant-number-of-files-about-users-ancestry\/","title":{"rendered":"23andMe says hackers accessed &#8216;significant number&#8217; of files about users&#8217; ancestry"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">Genetic testing company 23andMe announced on Friday that hackers accessed around 14,000 customer accounts in the company\u2019s recent data breach.<\/p>\n<p><a href=\"https:\/\/www.sec.gov\/ix?doc=\/Archives\/edgar\/data\/1804591\/000119312523287449\/d242666d8ka.htm\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">In a new filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission<\/a> published Friday, the company said that, based on its investigation into the incident, it had determined that hackers had accessed 0.1% of its customer base. <a href=\"https:\/\/investors.23andme.com\/news-releases\/news-release-details\/23andme-reports-fy2023-fourth-quarter-and-full-year-financial\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">According to the company&#8217;s most recent annual earnings report<\/a>, 23andMe has \u201cmore than 14 million customers worldwide,\u201d which means 0.1% is around 14,000.<\/p>\n<p>But the company also said that by accessing those accounts, the hackers were also able to access \u201ca significant number of files containing profile information about other users\u2019 ancestry that such users chose to share when opting in to 23andMe\u2019s DNA Relatives feature.\u201d<\/p>\n<p>The company did not specify what that \u201csignificant number\u201d of files is, nor how many of these \u201cother users\u201d were impacted.<\/p>\n<p>23andMe did not immediately respond to a request for comment, which included questions on those numbers.<\/p>\n<p>In early October, <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/10\/10\/23andme-resets-user-passwords-after-genetic-data-posted-online\/\">23andMe disclosed an incident<\/a> in which hackers had stolen some users\u2019 data using a common technique known as \u201ccredential stuffing,\u201d whereby cybercriminals hack into a victim&#8217;s account by using a known password, perhaps leaked due to a data breach on another service.<\/p>\n<p>The damage, however, did not stop with the customers who had their accounts accessed. 23andMe allows users to opt into a feature called <a href=\"https:\/\/customercare.23andme.com\/hc\/en-us\/articles\/212170838\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">DNA Relatives<\/a>. If a user opts-in to that feature, 23andMe shares some of that user\u2019s information with others. That means that by accessing one victim\u2019s account, hackers were also able to see the personal data of people connected to that initial victim.<\/p>\n<p>23andMe said in the filing that for the initial 14,000 users, the stolen data \u201cgenerally included ancestry information, and, for a subset of those accounts, health-related information based upon the user\u2019s genetics.\u201d For the other subset of users, 23andMe only said that the hackers stole \u201cprofile information\u201d and then posted unspecified \u201ccertain information\u201d online.<\/p>\n<p>TechCrunch analyzed the published sets of stolen data by comparing it to known public genealogy records, including websites published by hobbyists and genealogists. Although the sets of data were formatted differently, they contained some of the same unique user and genetic information that matched genealogy records published online years earlier.<\/p>\n<p>The owner of one genealogy website, for which some of their relatives&#8217; information was exposed in 23andMe&#8217;s data breach, told TechCrunch that they have about 5,000 relatives discovered through 23andMe, and said our &#8220;correlations might take that into account.&#8221;<\/p>\n<p>News of the data breach <a href=\"https:\/\/www.wired.com\/story\/23andme-credential-stuffing-data-stolen\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">surfaced online<\/a> in October when hackers advertised the alleged data of one million users of Jewish Ashkenazi descent and 100,000 Chinese users on a well-known hacking forum. Roughly two weeks later, the same hacker who advertised the initial stolen user data <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/10\/18\/hacker-leaks-millions-more-23andme-user-records-on-cybercrime-forum\/\">advertised the alleged records of four million more people<\/a>. The hacker was trying to sell the data of individual victims for $1 to $10.<\/p>\n<p>TechCrunch found that another hacker on a different hacking forum <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/10\/10\/hackers-advertised-23andme-stolen-data-two-months-ago\/\">had advertised even more allegedly stolen user data two months before<\/a> the advertisement that was initially reported by news outlets in October. In that first advertisement, the hacker claimed to have 300 terabytes of stolen 23andMe user data, and asked for $50 million to sell the whole database, or between $1,000 and $10,000 for a subset of the data.<\/p>\n<p>In response to the data breach, on October 10, 23andMe forced users to reset and change their passwords and encouraged them to turn on multi-factor authentication. And on November 6, the company required all users to use two-step verification, according to the new filing.<\/p>\n<p>After the 23andMe breach, <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/07\/23andme-ancestry-myheritage-two-factor-by-default\/\">other DNA testing companies Ancestry and MyHeritage<\/a> started mandating two-factor authentication.<\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>Genetic testing company 23andMe announced on Friday that hackers accessed around 14,000 customer accounts in the company\u2019s recent data breach. In a new filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission published Friday, the company said that, based on its investigation into the incident, it had determined that hackers had accessed 0.1% of its customer [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":133574594,"featured_media":2636848,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"91ac2367-81de-3b8b-add7-b5d9e6c8c36d","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-12-01T22:43:10Z","apple_news_api_id":"c5bd1269-fbf2-4268-b94f-73a1b33f0581","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T23:19:29Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/apple.news\/Axb0SafvyQmi5T3Ohsz8FgQ","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[21587494],"tags":[1156876,51751,965824,67372,61188,1816,190980,78192],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- This site is optimized with the Yoast SEO Premium plugin v20.2.1 (Yoast SEO v20.2.1) - https:\/\/yoast.com\/wordpress\/plugins\/seo\/ -->\n<title>23andMe says hackers accessed &#039;significant number&#039; of files about users&#039; ancestry | TechCrunch<\/title>\n<meta name=\"description\" content=\"23andMe revealed new details about its data breach. nch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=78192"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=78192"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=78192"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=78192"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636777,"date":"2023-12-01T14:08:57","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T22:08:57","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636777"},"modified":"2023-12-01T14:59:13","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T22:59:13","slug":"openais-gpt-store-delayed-to-2024-following-leadership-chaos","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/openais-gpt-store-delayed-to-2024-following-leadership-chaos\/","title":{"rendered":"OpenAI&#8217;s GPT Store delayed to 2024 following leadership chaos"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">OpenAI&#8217;s app store for AI, the GPT Store, will not launch this year as previously announced, but rather on an unspecified date in early 2024, the company said. The delay is almost certainly due to the leadership shakeup that occurred in November, just after the initial announcement.<\/p>\n<p>The news was <a href=\"https:\/\/www.axios.com\/2023\/12\/01\/openai-delays-launch-custom-gpt-store-2024\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">first reported by Axios<\/a>, which obtained the memo sent to users and developers. It read in part: &#8220;<span class=\"commtext c00\">we are now planning to launch the GPT Store early next year<\/span>. While we had expected to release it this month, a few unexpected things have been keeping us busy!&#8221; It also detailed some upcoming improvements to the new feature, like a better configuration interface and debug messages.<\/p>\n<p>I have contacted OpenAI and others for further information and will update if they respond.<\/p>\n<p>The launch of the store this month raised eyebrows when it was officially announced at OpenAI&#8217;s Dev Day conference in November. Though there was a working mockup of the store itself and a few of the fine-tuned models called GPTs available to inspect, there were a lot of unanswered questions.<\/p>\n<p>In a Q&amp;A with briefly dethroned CEO Sam Altman and CTO Mira Murati, I asked what the company&#8217;s plan was for the store as far as charging customers and paying developers. Their answer was, essentially, &#8220;we&#8217;ll see.&#8221;<\/p>\n<div class=\"embed breakout embed-oembed embed--techcrunch\">\n<blockquote class=\"wp-embedded-content\" data-secret=\"cikVeqjbyu\"><p><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/06\/app-store-for-ai-build-your-own-gpt-and-sell-it-on-openais-gpt-store\/\">App Store for AI: OpenAI&#8217;s GPT Store lets you build (and monetize) your own GPT<\/a><\/p><\/blockquote>\n<p><iframe class=\"wp-embedded-content\" sandbox=\"allow-scripts\" security=\"restricted\" style=\"position: absolute; clip: rect(1px, 1px, 1px, 1px);\" title=\"&#8220;App Store for AI: OpenAI&#8217;s GPT Store lets you build (and monetize) your own GPT&#8221; &#8212; TechCrunch\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/06\/app-store-for-ai-build-your-own-gpt-and-sell-it-on-openais-gpt-store\/embed\/#?secret=AHbO60Xoz7#?secret=cikVeqjbyu\" data-secret=\"cikVeqjbyu\" width=\"800\" height=\"450\" frameborder=\"0\" marginwidth=\"0\" marginheight=\"0\" scrolling=\"no\"><\/iframe><\/div>\n<p>Given how loose the launch plan was, and that a full week of work at the least was likely lost to the brief but intense battle of board versus CEO, the delay is not much of a surprise. With winter holidays approaching, the idea of shipping a major product under duress while simultaneously navigating a new board and other priorities was probably a non-starter.<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<p>For OpenAI customers, GPTs can still be made and shared with others directly, but they will not be publicly listed or able to take part in any revenue-sharing scheme (if OpenAI decides how to implement one) until the store formally launches.<\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>OpenAI&#8217;s app store for AI, the GPT Store, will not launch this year as previously announced, but rather on an unspecified date in early 2024, the company said. The delay is almost certainly due to the leadership shakeup that occurred in November, just after the initial announcement. The news was first reported by Axios, which [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":12084691,"featured_media":2625182,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"e46aede9-351f-3d3e-89cb-d33b5a31f17a","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-12-01T22:09:04Z","apple_news_api_id":"f5069910-b2a6-44b6-a0f8-17c2f456c705","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T22:59:06Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/apple.news\/A9QaZELKmRLag-BfC9FbHBQ","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[577047203],"tags":[577089886,421223872],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- coverage on artificial intelligence and machine learning tech, the companies building them, and the ethical issues AI raises today. This encompasses generative AI, including large language models, text-to-image and text-to-video models; speech recognition and generation; and predictive /techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=421223872"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=421223872"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=421223872"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=421223872"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636715,"date":"2023-12-01T11:24:45","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T19:24:45","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636715"},"modified":"2023-12-01T11:41:05","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T19:41:05","slug":"good-old-fashioned-ai-remains-viable-in-spite-of-the-rise-of-llms","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/good-old-fashioned-ai-remains-viable-in-spite-of-the-rise-of-llms\/","title":{"rendered":"Good old-fashioned AI remains viable in spite of the rise of LLMs"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">Remember a year ago, all the way back <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/30\/on-chatgpts-first-anniversary-its-mobile-apps-have-topped-110m-installs-and-nearly-30m-in-revenue\/\">to last November <\/a>before we knew about ChatGPT, when machine learning was all about building models to solve for a single task like loan approvals or fraud protection? That approach seemed to go out the window with the rise of generalized LLMs, but the fact is generalized models aren\u2019t well suited to every problem, and task-based models are still alive and well in the enterprise.<\/p>\n<p>These task-based models have, up until the rise of LLMs, been the basis for most AI in the enterprise, and they aren\u2019t going away. It\u2019s what Amazon CTO Werner Vogels referred to as \u201cgood old-fashioned AI\u201d in his keynote this week, and in his view, is the kind of AI that is still solving a lot of real-world problems.<\/p>\n<p>Atul Deo, general manager of Amazon Bedrock, the product <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/09\/28\/amazon-launches-its-bedrock-generative-ai-service-in-general-availability\/\">introduced earlier this year<\/a> as a way to plug into a variety of large language models via APIs, also believes that task models aren\u2019t going to simply disappear. Instead, they have become another AI tool in the arsenal.<\/p>\n<p>\u201cBefore the advent of large language models, we were mostly in a task-specific world. And the idea there was you would train a model from scratch for a particular task,\u201d Deo told TechCrunch. He says the main difference between the task model and the LLM is that one is trained for that specific task, while the other can handle things outside the boundaries of the model.<\/p>\n<p>Jon Turow, a partner at investment firm Madrona, who formerly spent almost a decade at AWS, says the industry has been talking about emerging capabilities in large language models like reasoning and out-of-domain robustness. \u201cThese allow you to be able to stretch beyond a narrow definition of what the model was initially expected to do,\u201d he said. But, he added, it\u2019s still very much up for debate how far these capabilities can go.<\/p>\n<p>Like Deo, Turow says task models aren\u2019t simply going to suddenly go away. \u201cThere is clearly still a role for task-specific models because they can be smaller, they can be faster, they can be cheaper and they can in some cases even be more performant because they&#8217;re designed for a specific task,\u201d he said.<\/p>\n<p>But the lure of an all-purpose model is hard to ignore. \u201cWhen you&#8217;re looking at an aggregate level in a company, when there are hundreds of machine learning models being trained separately, that doesn&#8217;t make any sense,\u201d Deo said. \u201cWhereas if you went with a more capable large language model, you get the reusability benefit right away, while allowing you to use a single model to tackle a bunch of different use cases.&#8221;<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<p>For Amazon, SageMaker, the company&#8217;s machine learning operations platform, remains a key product, one that is aimed at data scientists instead of developers, as Bedrock is.<a href=\"https:\/\/aws.amazon.com\/blogs\/machine-learning\/aws-celebrates-5-years-of-innovation-with-amazon-sagemaker\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">\u00a0It reports<\/a> tens of thousands of customers building millions of models. It would be foolhardy to give that up, and frankly just because LLMs are the flavor of the moment doesn\u2019t mean that the technology that came before won&#8217;t remain relevant for some time to come.<\/p>\n<p>Enterprise software in particular doesn\u2019t work that way. Nobody is simply tossing their significant investment because a new thing came along, even one as powerful as the current crop of large language models. It&#8217;s worth noting that Amazon did <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/amazon-sagemaker-hyperpod-makes-it-easier-to-train-and-fine-tune-llms\/\">announce upgrades to SageMaker<\/a> this week, aimed squarely at managing large language models.<\/p>\n<p>Prior to these more capable large language models, the task model was really the only option, and that\u2019s how companies approached it, by building a team of data scientists to help develop these models. What is the role of the data scientist in the age of large language models where tools are being aimed at developers? Turow thinks they still have a key job to do, even in companies concentrating on LLMs.<\/p>\n<p>\u201cThey&#8217;re going to think critically about data, and that is actually a role that is growing, not shrinking,\u201d he said. Regardless of the model, Turow believes data scientists will help people understand the relationship between AI and data inside large companies.<\/p>\n<p>\u201cI think every one of us needs to really think critically about what AI is and is not capable of and what data does and does not mean,\u201d he said. And that\u2019s true regardless of whether you\u2019re building a more generalized large language model or a task model.<\/p>\n<p>That&#8217;s why these two approaches will continue to work concurrently for some time to come because sometimes bigger is better, and sometimes it\u2019s not.<\/p>\n<p><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/tag\/aws-reinvent-2023\/\"><img decoding=\"async\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/aws-reinvent-2023-banner.jpg\" alt=\"Read more about AWS re:Invent 2023 on TechCrunch\" \/><\/a><\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>Remember a year ago, all the way back to last November before we knew about ChatGPT, when machine learning was all about building models to solve for a single task like loan approvals or fraud protection? That approach seemed to go out the window with the rise of generalized LLMs, but the fact is generalized [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":521068,"featured_media":2636724,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"d6eab132-8fbb-3340-9c5e-bc57908e5f9f","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-12-01T19:24:52Z","apple_news_api_id":"b119ef43-1e13-4a16-afb0-5d1b45963ffb","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T19:40:54Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAQ==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/apple.news\/AsRnvQx4TShavsF0bRZY_-w","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[577047203,449557044],"tags":[577210801,576717904,577006963],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- end of Elon"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">Standing on the rear bed of his greatest boondoggle, his face obscured in shadow, the billionaire who built a rabid fanbase through seemingly inhuman feats of engineering and willpower cut a greatly reduced figure: Elon Musk was presiding over the first customer deliveries of a Tesla truck that, like most things Musk touched, was drastically over budget and far beyond its original shipping timeline. Plus, just a couple days earlier Musk had essentially sealed the dismal fate of another of his companies by<a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/elon-musk-says-go-fuck-yourself-to-advertisers-leaving-x\/\"> publicly telling advertising partners to &#8220;go fuck [themselves].&#8221;<\/a><\/p>\n<p>To say he was looking diminished is putting it mildly.<\/p>\n<p>The Cybertruck launch, like most of Tesla&#8217;s delivery events, was not a substantive affair. My more patient colleagues have done a much better job actually <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/30\/tesla-cybertruck-delivery-event-everything-elon-revealed-about-the-ev-pickup\/\">recounting what happened there than I ever could<\/a>. Suffice it to say that Musk touted his gaudy stainless steel monstrosity&#8217;s abilities for a while before explaining to a handful of the sycophantic first new Cybertruck owners (including one Alexis Ohanian) the extremely non-obvious way to open their doors and then repeating a vague and perplexing statement about how wild it&#8217;s going to be to see these driving around before calling it a day.<\/p>\n<p>I mentioned the odd choice to stand on the truck bed in semi-darkness despite having a stage lighting setup very clearly designed for him not to do that, but I neglected to note the moment when a sheepish Franz von Holzhausen was drug out to weakly toss a baseball at the Cybertruck&#8217;s windows, in a moment that seemed like unintended satire of when he <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2019\/11\/21\/tesla-accidentally-busted-two-windows-on-the-cybertruck-while-demonstrating-how-tough-they-are\/\">smashed the windows of the original prototype at a reveal event in 2019<\/a>. The only takeaway from this watered down reenactment was that if von Holzhausen had replicated the 2019 mishap faithfully, with steel balls and a proper toss, I&#8217;m pretty much convinced he would&#8217;ve smashed the windows again.<\/p>\n<p>Musk didn&#8217;t address his crumbling social media product or how his frequent, inflammatory comments and amplification of false and dangerous conspiracy theories have made it impossible to see a path forward for X that doesn&#8217;t end badly. He didn&#8217;t talk about how his range of distractions, including a repeatedly exploding spacecraft he&#8217;s periodically launching from south Texas, are giving Tesla investors some serious misgivings. Mostly, he talked about the Cybertruck&#8217;s potential benefits to owners in case of the end of modern civilization. And again: about how cool they&#8217;ll look.<\/p>\n<p>No longer the confident, if controversial, prognosticator and big swing solutions engineer, Musk has instead become a mad-eyed, rambling and raving advocate of far-right nonsense and vacillating convictions. Even his seemingly existential objection to OpenAI&#8217;s fast-paced development of AI ended up being little more than petty jealousy, as evidenced by the harried introduction of Grok, his own, far more puerile and muddy-minded equivalent.<\/p>\n<p>Undoubtedly, Musk continues to benefit from legions of devoted fanboys &#8212; many of whom I&#8217;m sure will be letting me know how amazing he still is and how garbage I still am &#8212; but it&#8217;s hard to deny any longer that his influence is on the wane, and the crowning moment of his stupidest idea made flesh in the form of the Cybertruck seems like a meaningful bookend on his era of power.<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<p>&nbsp;<\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>Standing on the rear bed of his greatest boondoggle, his face obscured in shadow, the billionaire who built a rabid fanbase through seemingly inhuman feats of engineering and willpower cut a greatly reduced figure: Elon Musk was presiding over the first customer deliveries of a Tesla truck that, like most things Musk touched, was drastically [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":5302483,"featured_media":2636578,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"7552398d-df8c-3fa2-92c3-e57ab2c9aeca","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"","apple_news_api_id":"","apple_news_api_modified_at":"","apple_news_api_revision":"","apple_news_api_share_url":"","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[2401],"tags":[576716027,1244446,429989,576716026],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- /techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=576716026"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=576716026"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=576716026"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=576716026"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636538,"date":"2023-12-01T09:30:56","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T17:30:56","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636538"},"modified":"2023-12-01T10:12:42","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T18:12:42","slug":"rover-spac-sale-price","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/rover-spac-sale-price\/","title":{"rendered":"Why Rover&#8217;s $2.3B sale price makes good sense"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\"><span class=\"featured__span-first-words\">Rover is going<\/span> private in a $2.3 billion, all-cash sale to Blackstone, the company announced earlier this week. The pet care\u2013focused company raised <a href=\"https:\/\/www.crunchbase.com\/organization\/rover-com\/company_financials\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">hundreds of millions of dollars<\/a> while private, through a Series G, and later <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2021\/02\/16\/inside-rover-and-moneylions-spac-led-public-debuts\/\">went public via a SPAC<\/a>. Notably, unlike a great many SPAC combinations, Rover is proving that blank-check companies are not merely a way to incinerate wealth.<\/p>\n<hr \/>\n<p style=\"text-align: center;\"><strong><em>The Exchange explores startups, markets and money. <\/em><\/strong><\/p>\n<p style=\"text-align: center;\"><strong><em>Read it <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/subscribe\/?tpcc=theexchange\">every morning on TechCrunch+<\/a> or get <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/newsletters\/?utm_source=internal&amp;utm_medium=WPunit\">The Exchange newsletter<\/a> every Saturday.<\/em><\/strong><\/p>\n<hr \/>\n<p>The former startup has a 30-day shopping provision built into its deal with Blackstone, meaning that other offers may come to the fore. However, with the private equity group paying a stiff premium for Rover shares \u2014 61% more than the company&#8217;s 90-day volume weighted average share price, <a href=\"https:\/\/www.blackstone.com\/news\/press\/rover-agrees-to-be-acquired-by-blackstone-in-2-3-billion-transaction\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">per a release<\/a> \u2014 that doesn&#8217;t sound too likely.<\/p>\n<p><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/tag\/the-exchange\/\"><img fetchpriority=\"high\" decoding=\"async\" class=\"alignright wp-image-2220369 size-medium\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?w=300\" alt=\"\" width=\"300\" height=\"300\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg 900w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=150,150 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=300,300 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=768,768 768w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=680,680 680w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=32,32 32w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=50,50 50w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=64,64 64w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=96,96 96w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2021\/10\/exchange-banner-sq-orng-plus.jpg?resize=128,128 128w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px\" \/><\/a>The Rover deal is expensive but it has some notable caveats that tell us quite a lot about the state of the market for tech, and tech-enabled, companies. I&#8217;ll bet you didn&#8217;t expect a pet-focused e-commerce marketplace to earn an 8.7x revenue run rate multiple in 2023!<\/p>\n<p>This morning I want to dig into why I think Blackstone is paying so much for Rover, what we can glean from that research for other startups, and why a select few SPAC&#8217;d public companies that are trading like literal dogs may be bargains for the right buyer.<\/p>\n<h2>That price tag isn&#8217;t unreasonable<\/h2>\n<p>Rover and Blackstone announced their transaction after we got the former <a href=\"https:\/\/investors.rover.com\/news-releases\/news-release-details\/rover-reports-third-quarter-2023-financial-results\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">company&#8217;s Q3 2023 results<\/a>, meaning that we have pretty up-to-date figures concerning its recent performance.<\/p>\n<p>Rover reported third-quarter revenues of $66.2 million, up 30% from the same period one year ago. The company also flipped to GAAP net income and told investors that it intended to buy back more of its own shares. It also <a href=\"https:\/\/www.investorsobserver.com\/news\/stock-update\/rover-group-inc-up-23-65-to-8-00-after-earnings-beat\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">beat guidance<\/a> in the quarter.<div class=\"extra-crunch-offer-container\"><\/div>","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>The Rover deal is expensive, but it has some notable caveats that tell us quite a lot about the state of the market for tech, and tech-enabled, companies.<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":428363,"featured_media":2621031,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"2d6e9d10-1a36-38b5-8ae0-2e959cef7c2e","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"","apple_news_api_id":"","apple_news_api_modified_at":"","apple_news_api_revision":"","apple_news_api_share_url":"","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[20429],"tags":[577025428,29172,576765839],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[576796356],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- Link","children":[]}]},"entities":{"posts":[{"id":2636827,"date":"2023-12-01T14:25:05","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T22:25:05","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636827"},"modified":"2023-12-01T15:09:06","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T23:09:06","slug":"ev-startup-fisker-cut-its-2023-production-target-for-the-fourth-time","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/ev-startup-fisker-cut-its-2023-production-target-for-the-fourth-time\/","title":{"rendered":"Ev startup Fisker cut its 2023 production target for the fourth time"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">Fisker, the California-based EV startup, cut its annual production guidance in an effort to free up $300 million in working capital, the company said in a business update Friday.<\/p>\n<p>Fisker said it expects to produce about 10,000 vehicles this year. The decision comes less than a month since Fisker cut its production target to between 13,000 and 17,000 vehicles for 2023. The production guidance is just a quarter of Fisker&#8217;s bullish forecast from a year ago. In November 2022, Fisker said it planned to produce <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2022\/11\/02\/fisker-bumps-up-production-for-all-electric-ocean-suv\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">42,400 Ocean SUVs<\/a> by the end of 2023 due to strong demand in the U.S. and Europe. That rosy projection was slashed in May to 32,000-36,000 vehicles and then cut again in August to 20,000-23,000 vehicles. This latest update makes four reductions since the spring.<\/p>\n<p>The production cut will allow the company to access $300 million in working capital, giving the company &#8220;flexibility,&#8221; according to the business update.<\/p>\n<p>&#8220;Our teams have worked hard to overcome some early delivery challenges and are now setting an impressive pace as we prepare to close out 2023,&#8221; Chairman and CEO Henrik Fisker said in a statement. &#8220;We may not have hit our original forecast but taking current market conditions and negative sentiments around EV sales into account, I would say we are doing quite well, as we continue to accelerate sales and deliveries. This is yielding considerable revenue as we ramp up our business. I expect by the end of this year we will have delivered more customer cars than any Western EV startup did in their first year of deliveries. The company continues to sharpen its focus on growing its current markets and enhancing our sales and service offerings for the Fisker Ocean.&#8221;<\/p>\n<p>Fisker said <a href=\"https:\/\/investors.fiskerinc.com\/news\/news-details\/2023\/Fisker-Provides-Business-Update\/default.aspx\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">in its business update<\/a> that it has also launched a new strategy to improve deliveries in the U.S. and Europe, which helped it overcome early logistics hurdles. While Fisker didn&#8217;t elaborate on exactly what those challenges were, it appears the strategy involves adding more transportation logistics companies to speed up deliveries, increased outreach to reservation holders and opening more facilities dedicated to retail, deliveries and service.<\/p>\n<p>The company said it&#8217;s also launching a leasing program in the U.S., Canada and Europe, but didn&#8217;t include details on when that might occur.<\/p>\n<p>Fisker also provided an update around hiring, importantly Dan Quirk as its new executive vice president of finance and accounting. The hiring comes after Fisker lost two chief accounting officers in short succession and delayed the filing of its quarterly earnings report with the Securities and Exchange Commission. Other hires include Axel Buhr as vice president of finance and controller operations, Ram Iyer as senior VP of EE integration and validation and Wolfgang Hoffmann as country manager in Canada, where Fisker is about to begin deliveries.<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>Fisker, the California-based EV startup, cut its annual production guidance in an effort to free up $300 million in working capital, the company said in a business update Friday. Fisker said it expects to produce about 10,000 vehicles this year. The decision comes less than a month since Fisker cut its production target to between [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":133574188,"featured_media":2566477,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"8ab52a9f-f276-3d6d-99b5-b3d7248867f4","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-12-01T22:25:10Z","apple_news_api_id":"0f379758-3879-440c-9165-d92cdf4c9e3c","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T23:09:00Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/apple.news\/ADzeXWDh5RAyRZdks30yePA","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[20429,2401],"tags":[197443,76801,495498],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- This site is optimized with the Yoast SEO Premium plugin v20.2.1 (Yoast SEO v20.2.1) - https:\/\/yoast.com\/wordpress\/plugins\/seo\/ -->\n<title>Ev startup Fisker cut its 2023 production target for the fourth time | TechCrunch<\/title>\n<meta name=\"description\" content=\"California EV startup Fisker has cut its production guidance again for 2023. The company now expects to deliver about 10,000 EVs.\" \/>\n<link rel=\"canonical\" href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/ev-startup-fisker-cut-its-2023-production-target-for-the-fourth-time\/\" \/>\n<meta property=\"og:locale\" content=\"en_US\" \/>\n<meta property=\"og:type\" content=\"article\" \/>\n<meta property=\"og:title\" content=\"Ev startup Fisker cut its 2023 production target for the fourth time | TechCrunch\" \/>\n<meta property=\"og:description\" content=\"California EV startup Fisker has cut its production guidance again for 2023. The company now expects to deliver about 10,000 EVs.\" \/>\n<meta property=\"og:url\" content=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/ev-startup-fisker-cut-its-2023-production-target-for-the-fourth-time\/\" \/>\n<meta property=\"og:site_name\" content=\"TechCrunch\" \/>\n<meta runch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=495498"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=495498"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=495498"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=495498"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636200,"date":"2023-12-01T11:50:35","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T19:50:35","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636200"},"modified":"2023-12-01T12:00:24","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T20:00:24","slug":"tesla-cybertruck-delivery-event-everything-elon-revealed-about-the-ev-pickup","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/tesla-cybertruck-delivery-event-everything-elon-revealed-about-the-ev-pickup\/","title":{"rendered":"Tesla Cybertruck delivery event: Everything Elon revealed about the EV pickup"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">With dubstep as the soundtrack and neon lighting as the backdrop, Elon Musk handed the first Cybertrucks over to a select group of customers that included Reddit co-founder and VC fund Seven Seven Six founder Alexis Ohanian and Trousdale Ventures founder and CEO Phillip Sarofim.<\/p>\n<p>The livestreamed portion of the <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/30\/whats-up-with-teslas-cybertruck-everything-to-know-about-the-much-hyped-electric-pickup\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Tesla Cybertruck delivery event<\/a> was a short affair \u2014 around 30 minutes. But the event still had all the traditional trappings one has come to expect from Tesla: the pomp and pumpy music, VIP guests and, of course, Musk.<\/p>\n<p>The Tesla Cybertruck deliveries come at least six years since Musk first tweeted about building a truck and four years since he debuted the futuristic-looking pickup.<\/p>\n<p><span style=\"font-size: 1rem; letter-spacing: -0.1px;\">Looking beyond some of the flashier features \u2014 it&#8217;s bullet proof \u2014 here&#8217;s everything we know so far. The tl;dr: the Cybertruck is a lot more expensive than the targets Musk shared in 2019. And what other details were shared, were scant. A few reviews, including <a href=\"https:\/\/www.youtube.com\/watch?v=XxOh12Uhg08\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">one from Marques Brownlee<\/a>, have been far more illuminating than what Musk or the official Tesla website have shared.<\/span><\/p>\n<h2>Three versions<\/h2>\n<p><div id=\"attachment_2636304\" style=\"width: 1034px\" class=\"wp-caption alignnone\"><a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg\"><img fetchpriority=\"high\" decoding=\"async\" aria-describedby=\"caption-attachment-2636304\" class=\"size-full wp-image-2636304\" src=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg\" alt=\"Tesla cybertruck delivery event\" width=\"1024\" height=\"477\" srcset=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg 2934w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=150,70 150w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=300,140 300w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=768,358 768w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=680,317 680w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=1536,715 1536w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=2048,953 2048w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=1200,559 1200w, https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2023\/11\/Screenshot-2023-11-30-at-1.25.54-PM.jpg?resize=50,23 50w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px\" \/><\/a><p id=\"caption-attachment-2636304\" class=\"wp-caption-text\"><strong>Image Credits:<\/strong> Tesla\/screenshot<\/p><\/div><\/p>\n<p>The Tesla Cybertruck will eventually be available in three configurations.<\/p>\n<p>The cheapest of the batch, a single-motor rear-wheel version with 250 miles of range, a 6.9-second zero to 60 miles per hour acceleration rate and a $60,990 base price, <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/30\/teslas-cheapest-cybertruck-wont-drop-until-2025\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">won&#8217;t be available until 2025<\/a>. That leaves a dual motor all-wheel version and the so-called Cyberbeast, which has three motors.<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<p>The all-wheel drive variant has an estimated 340-mile range, top speed of 112 mph and $79,990 starting price. The Cyberbeast comes with an estimated 320-mile range model with 845 horsepower that can travel 0 to 60 miles per hour in 2.6 seconds, can hit a top speed of 130 mph, and comes with estimated $99,990 price tag. Both of these versions have a claimed towing capacity of 11,000 pounds.<\/p>\n<p>The company is also going to offer a range extender that will push the all-wheel drive version to an estimated 470 miles and the Cyberbeast to more than 440 miles of range. But Tesla provides no other details about the range extender or the pricing. After the event, Musk did take to X, formerly Twitter, to give a slightly less opaque explanation. He said the range extender will be an &#8220;optional pack that fits in about 1\/3 of the truck bed. Still room for plenty of cargo. It\u2019s meant for very long trips or towing heavy things up mountains.&#8221;<\/p>\n<p>Still no word on what it will cost. Either way, any EV truck or SUV priced above $80,000 won&#8217;t qualify for the $7,500 federal tax credit. Which means that middle tier variant with a range extender probably won&#8217;t.<\/p>\n<p><span style=\"font-size: 1rem; letter-spacing: -0.1px;\">The numbers above differ from the original specs first shared by Musk at the <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2019\/11\/21\/behold-the-tesla-cybertruck-is-here\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Cybertruck debut event<\/a> in 2019. The company was planning on three variants, but the prices, towing and range have since changed. The production vehicle dimensions have also been reduced by about 5% from the prototype shown four years ago, making it slightly smaller than the Ford F-150 Lightning. <\/span><span style=\"font-size: 1rem; letter-spacing: -0.1px;\">In 2019, the company planned for its cheapest version to cost $39,900, and have a towing capacity of 7,500 pounds and more than 250 miles of range. The middle version was slated to be a dual-motor all-wheel drive priced at $49,900, have a towing capacity of more than 10,000 pounds and be able to travel more than 300 miles on a single charge. The third version was supposed to have three electric motors and all-wheel drive, a towing capacity of 14,000 pounds and battery range of more than 500 miles. That version, known as \u201ctri motor,\u201d is priced at $69,900.<\/span><\/p>\n<div class=\"text-loader--content tds--vertical_padding--small tds-o-text--caption\" tabindex=\"-1\">\n<p>Today, all of the production Cybertrucks have a drag coefficient of 0.335 (that&#8217;s good aerodynamics, fyi), 35-inch all-terrain tires on 20-inch rims, a six-foot by four-foot composite truck bed, a hidden gear locker and a front trunk. The company says that in all there is 67 cubic feet of lockable storage and it has a maximum payload of 2,500 pounds. Notably, the Cybertruck is a <span style=\"font-size: 1rem; letter-spacing: -0.1px;\">steer-by-wire vehicle, which should give it the ability to make tight turns and maneuvers.\u00a0<\/span><\/p>\n<div class=\"text-loader--content tds--vertical_padding--small tds-o-text--caption\" tabindex=\"-1\">\n<p><span style=\"font-size: 1rem; letter-spacing: -0.1px;\">Also of note, Tesla switched from a 12-volt to a 48-volt electrical system like other automakers such as Mercedes-Benz and Volkswagen have done. The 48-volt system delivers more efficient power distribution and can provide enough amperage to charge things like power tools.\u00a0\u00a0<\/span><\/p>\n<\/div>\n<div class=\"text-loader--content tds--vertical_padding--small tds-o-text--caption\" tabindex=\"-1\">\n<p>The Cybertruck also comes equipped with electronically adaptive air suspension that allows for up to 17 inches of clearance, if needed.<\/p>\n<\/div>\n<h2>Tesla Cybertruck exterior<\/h2>\n<p>The Tesla Cybertruck still has the same trapezoidal shape and stainless steel exterior as presented back in 2019. There are a few changes however. The truck bed is no longer stainless steel, but lined with a composite. And those side mirrors? They are removable.<\/p>\n<p>The exterior includes armored glass that is supposed to resist the impact of a baseball at 70 mph or class 4 hail. Testing the glass <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2019\/11\/21\/tesla-accidentally-busted-two-windows-on-the-cybertruck-while-demonstrating-how-tough-they-are\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">didn&#8217;t work so well back in 2019,<\/a> with it actually shattering. This time chief designer Franz von Holzhausen successfully threw a baseball at the vehicle&#8217;s window without breaking it. Although it should be noted that back in 2019, von Holzhausen threw a metal baseball-sized ball. On Thursday, it appeared to be a regular old baseball and not one thrown 70 mph.<\/p>\n<\/div>\n<div class=\"text-loader--content tds--vertical_padding--small tds-o-text--caption\" tabindex=\"-1\">\n<h2>Inside the Cybertruck<\/h2>\n<p>Now that <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/innovation-or-folly-the-cybertruck-will-test-whether-anyone-still-trusts-tesla\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Tesla Cybertrucks<\/a> are being delivered, there&#8217;s more information on some of the interior details and features.<\/p>\n<p>As Musk handed over the Cybertrucks, he pressed what looked like a button on the B pillar to open the doors. It should be noted that the backdoors open at 90 degrees, a detail that wasn&#8217;t pointed out at the event but was revealed in a few of the review videos that have since been released. Once inside, drivers and passengers might notice an 18.5-inch infinity touchscreen in the front and a 9.4-inch touchscreen in the back. Apparently, there&#8217;s also a new interface, although Tesla doesn&#8217;t elaborate on how it differs from the current version.<\/p>\n<p>Unlike the prototype, the steering wheel is a wheel and not a yoke. And another oddly placed item is the gear shifter, in this case buttons that allow the driver to move from park to reserve or drive, is placed up by where one would normally find the rearview mirror. Oh and yup, there is no rearview mirror.<\/p>\n<p>Interestingly, and as noted by Brownlee, there is a front-facing camera outside of the Cybertruck \u2014 a first in Tesla&#8217;s world \u2014 which projects an image to that center screen to help the driver see any potential obstacles.<\/p>\n<p>The Cybertruck also comes with a 15-speaker sound system that includes two dedicated subwoofers and distributed amplifiers. A built-in HEPA air filter (also in other Tesla vehicles), a wireless charging system and 65W USB-C and 120V\/240V outlets are also included.<\/p>\n<\/div>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>With dubstep as the soundtrack and neon lighting as the backdrop, Elon Musk handed the first Cybertrucks over to a select group of customers that included Reddit co-founder and VC fund Seven Seven Six founder Alexis Ohanian and Trousdale Ventures founder and CEO Phillip Sarofim. The livestreamed portion of the Tesla Cybertruck delivery event was [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":133574188,"featured_media":2636252,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"a346e424-4f23-3928-8162-be8784969443","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-11-30T22:14:41Z","apple_news_api_id":"6f5619b7-f3ca-43be-8a46-295d85f2b083","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-12-01T20:00:29Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAEA==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/apple.news\/Ab1YZt_PKQ76KRildhfKwgw","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[2401],"tags":[576716027,197443,1244446,76801,429989],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- This site is optimized with the Yoast SEO Premium plugin v20.2.1 (Yoast SEO v20.2.1) - https:\/\/yoast.com\/wordpress\/plugins\/seo\/ -->\n<title>Tesla Cybertruck delivery event: Everything Elon revealed about the EV pickup | TechCrunch<\/title>\n<meta name=\"description\" content=\"Tesla CEO Elon Musk handed over the first Cybertrucks to customers at an event at its Texas gigafactory. runch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_battlefield?tags=429989"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc-media-gallery?tags=429989"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_unified_video?tags=429989"},{"href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/wp-json\/wp\/v2\/tc_video?tags=429989"}],"curies":[{"name":"wp","href":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/api.w.org\/{rel}","templated":true}]}}],[],[],[],[],[]]}},{"id":2636535,"date":"2023-12-01T07:05:57","date_gmt":"2023-12-01T15:05:57","guid":{"rendered":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/?p=2636535"},"modified":"2023-12-01T08:49:55","modified_gmt":"2023-12-01T16:49:55","slug":"big-wins-for-latin-america-climate-tech-momentum-and-rovers-2-3b-sale","status":"publish","type":"post","link":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/12\/01\/big-wins-for-latin-america-climate-tech-momentum-and-rovers-2-3b-sale\/","title":{"rendered":"Big wins for Latin America, climate tech momentum and Rover&#8217;s $2.3B sale"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\"><iframe class=\"embed-iframe\" src=\"https:\/\/player.simplecast.com\/6aa8ac3f-38a7-4615-afe7-58a3950c4289?dark=true\" width=\"100%\" height=\"200px\" frameborder=\"no\" scrolling=\"no\" seamless=\"\"><\/iframe><\/p>\n<p><em>Listen here or\u00a0<a href=\"https:\/\/link.chtbl.com\/Equity-q4-podposts?sid=Techcrunch\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\" data-mrf-link=\"https:\/\/link.chtbl.com\/Equity-q4-podposts?sid=Techcrunch\">wherever you get your podcasts<\/a>.<\/em><\/p>\n<p>Hello and welcome back to\u00a0<a href=\"https:\/\/podcasts.apple.com\/us\/podcast\/equity\/id1215439780\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\" data-mrf-link=\"https:\/\/podcasts.apple.com\/us\/podcast\/equity\/id1215439780\">Equity<\/a>, a podcast about the business of startups, where we unpack the numbers and nuance behind the headlines. This is our Friday show, and we\u2019re talking about the week\u2019s biggest startup and tech news.<\/p>\n<p>This week Mary Ann, Kirsten Korosec and Alex Wilhelm were on hand to chew through a veritable mountain of news. Despite the fact that today is the first of December, tech news is still dropping at a quick clip. So for now, there&#8217;s lots to talk about:<\/p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Self-driving setbacks:<\/strong> Cruise is set to <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/29\/gm-to-slash-spending-at-cruise-by-hundreds-of-millions-of-dollars\/\">slash its costs<\/a> in the wake of a safety mess and greater scrutiny from regulators. Alex hopes that the current set of news items doesn&#8217;t slow down the progress of self-driving tech.<\/li>\n<li><strong>Webull snaps up Flink:<\/strong> Free trading is not just a business model that is popular in the United States. Webull is taking the Latin American market more seriously with <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/webull-leaps-into-mexico-with-acquisition-of-stock-trading-app-flink\/\">its purchase of Flink<\/a>.<\/li>\n<li><strong>Uber &lt;3&#8217;s taxis:<\/strong> What are London&#8217;s iconic black cabs <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/londons-iconic-black-cabs-can-soon-be-hailed-on-uber\/\">doing on Uber&#8217;s platform<\/a>? And how long does it take to go from enemies to frenemies?<\/li>\n<li><strong>Rover finds the exit:<\/strong> Who would have guessed <a href=\"https:\/\/news.yahoo.com\/blackstone-acquires-pet-care-app-172112058.html?fr=sycsrp_catchall\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">that dog walking would be worth $2.3 billion<\/a>?<\/li>\n<li><strong>IPOs are heating back up (a little):<\/strong> With Shein and Reddit targeting the IPO market <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/28\/shein-reddit-ipo-plans\/\">in the coming months<\/a>, it&#8217;s time to get our S-1 boots back on and prepare for some new debuts.<\/li>\n<li><strong>Venture downturns are not death sentences:<\/strong> EU venture funding <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/27\/european-startup-funding-halved-to-42b-in-2023-says-atomico\/\">levels are down, but not out<\/a>. And <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/22\/australia-new-zealand-venture-capital\/\">what&#8217;s going on Down Under<\/a>?<\/li>\n<\/ul>\n<p>We are back on Monday! Chat then!<\/p>\n<div class=\"article-content\" data-mrf-recirculation=\"In-Article Links\">\n<div class=\"article-content\" data-mrf-recirculation=\"In-Article Links\">\n<div or folly? The Cybertruck will test whether anyone still trusts Tesla"},"content":{"rendered":"<p id=\"speakable-summary\">The Tesla Cybertruck, the first of which will be delivered Thursday four years after its debut, is loved and loathed. For fans, it&#8217;s a symbol for what Tesla and its CEO Elon Musk stand for: creativity, irreverence, rebellion. Others see it as an act of hubris. It could be both.<\/p>\n<p>Either way, the stakes are high. The automaker hasn&#8217;t produced a new passenger model \u2014 save for refreshes or variants of existing vehicles \u2014 in more than three years, and profit margins have shrunk due to price cuts aimed at preserving its market share. The Cybertruck could be Tesla&#8217;s magnum opus or its albatross.<\/p>\n<p>Where the Cybertruck lands in the history books (or more likely, Wikipedia) will depend on the company&#8217;s ability to navigate production woes and customer reception \u2014 particularly if it isn&#8217;t embraced lovingly once it&#8217;s in owners&#8217; hands. And of course, there&#8217;s the other hurdle of producing a vehicle at scale that people love <em>without<\/em> losing money.<\/p>\n<p>The next important step on this precarious journey begins at <a href=\"https:\/\/www.tesla.com\/cybertruck\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">2 p.m. CT November 30<\/a>, when Tesla is expected to deliver the first of its long-awaited Cybertrucks to patient customers. The angular, stainless steel, futuristic-looking trucks will be revealed and delivered during the Thursday afternoon event at the Tesla Gigafactory in Austin \u2014 and likely with all the pomp, circumstance and electronic dance music we&#8217;ve come to expect from Tesla.<\/p>\n<p>Some Tesla shareholders, Cybertruck customers and other VIP guests will attend in person. The event will be livestreamed for everyone else on a <a href=\"https:\/\/www.tesla.com\/cybertruck\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">special landing page<\/a> and likely on its\u00a0<a href=\"https:\/\/www.youtube.com\/@tesla\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">YouTube channel<\/a> and, of course, <a href=\"https:\/\/twitter.com\/Tesla?ref_src=twsrc%5Egoogle%7Ctwcamp%5Eserp%7Ctwgr%5Eauthor\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">on X<\/a>.<\/p>\n<h2>The stakes<\/h2>\n<p>If Tesla pulls off the Cybertruck, it might prove that it&#8217;s still the rebellious upstart at heart, with the vision and dedication to break the norm. It could also give its bottom line a much needed boost and forever differentiate it from legacy rivals.<\/p>\n<p>But the truck has faced many challenges and delays in getting to production. Musk has confessed that building the Cybertruck has been difficult due to its unique design and stainless-steel body, which has <a href=\"https:\/\/www.wsj.com\/business\/autos\/behind-teslas-challenges-in-making-the-cybertruck-ultrahard-stainless-steel-8cb4c1b9\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">reportedly<\/a> led to issues like gaps in between the panels. We don&#8217;t yet know how much the Cybertruck will cost, but Musk has warned it will take time before it&#8217;s a profitable vehicle for the automaker.<\/p><div class=\"piano-inline-promo\"><\/div>\n<p>During <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/10\/18\/tesla-q3-profits-fall-44-on-ev-price-cuts\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Tesla&#8217;s Q3 earnings call<\/a>, Musk told investors that Tesla &#8220;dug our own grave with the Cybertruck.&#8221; He said that scaling it will be tough and it will take at least 18 months <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/10\/18\/elon-musk-warns-cybertruck-wont-be-profitable-until-2025\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">before the pickup is profitable<\/a>. The truck has already taken a chunk out of Tesla&#8217;s earnings as the automaker&#8217;s operating expenses increased 43% year-over-year. Musk said that Giga Texas will be able to produce about 250,000 Cybertrucks a year starting in 2025, but his timelines are often skewed and unreliable.<\/p>\n<h2>Stainless steel frame: A risky bet<\/h2>\n<p>Musk&#8217;s goal for the Cybertruck was to be surprising, bold and to build something no one would expect because it didn&#8217;t look like any other pickup truck out there.<\/p>\n<p>&#8220;I don&#8217;t care if anyone buys it,&#8221; Musk said to his design team in 2019, according to Walter Isaacson&#8217;s biography of the billionaire executive. &#8220;We&#8217;re not doing a traditional boring truck. We can always do that later. I want to build something that&#8217;s cool. Like, don&#8217;t resist me.&#8221;<\/p>\n<p>As he and lead designer Franz von Holzhausen spitballed design ideas, they spoke about doing something revolutionary with the form and manufacturing process of the vehicle, which hadn&#8217;t changed for pickups in 80 years. That caused them to shift their focus to the material used to make it. Rethinking the material, and even the physics of the vehicle&#8217;s structure, opened their minds to new designs.<\/p>\n<p>After discussing the possibility of aluminum and titanium, they settled on stainless steel, according to information revealed in Isaacson&#8217;s biography. Charles Kuehmann, VP of materials engineering at both Tesla and SpaceX, had developed an ultra-hard stainless steel alloy that was &#8220;cold rolled&#8221; rather than requiring heat treatments. The team reasoned it was strong enough and cheap enough to use for both rocket ships and trucks. The steel body wouldn&#8217;t need to be painted, could resist dents and would be able to bear the vehicle&#8217;s structural load without relying on a chassis.<\/p>\n<p>&#8220;Let&#8217;s make the strength on the outside, make it an exoskeleton, and hang everything else from the inside of it,&#8221; Musk told his engineers.<\/p>\n<p>Building with stainless steel also meant that Tesla couldn&#8217;t use its stamping machines to sculpt carbon fiber into body panels with curves and shapes. The truck would have to be sharp and angular, which was fine with Musk, who had been inspired by vehicles like those in the video games Cyberpunk 2077 and Halo, as well as films like &#8220;Blade Runner&#8221; and &#8220;The Spy Who Loved Me.&#8221; In fact, Musk bought the 1970s Lotus Esprit that was used in the James Bond film for almost $1 million and displayed it in the Tesla design studio.<\/p>\n<p>The choice of using stainless steel created its own unforeseen problems, though, and has caused the delay of the Cybertruck&#8217;s launch. In theory, building a truck body with stainless steel panels should create a smooth, angular design. In practice, it&#8217;s difficult to get those panels to line up properly without exposing large gaps. It&#8217;s also hard to flatten the steel panels, <a href=\"https:\/\/www.wsj.com\/business\/autos\/behind-teslas-challenges-in-making-the-cybertruck-ultrahard-stainless-steel-8cb4c1b9\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">reports<\/a> The Wall Street Journal, citing people who have worked on the pickup. The metal is produced in coils, like giant rolls of paper towels, so when it&#8217;s unrolled, it has a tendency to spring back into its rolled-up form.<\/p>\n<p>Once the Cybertruck is on the road, customers might encounter challenges of their own. While the metal is likely to make it more resistant to dents and scratches, if it does get dented, fixing it will likely be a nightmare. Tesla already has a bad rep for inadequate servicing, with limited service centers, limited stock to replace parts, bad communications and long wait times for repair appointments. Given the difficulty of getting the Cybertruck to production at all, fixing one will probably be equally frustrating.<\/p>\n<h2>A bullet-proof truck?<\/h2>\n<p>During Tesla&#8217;s initial Cybertruck reveal event in 2019, Musk asked his lead designer, Franz von Holzhausen, to demonstrate the strength of the truck&#8217;s &#8220;armor glass&#8221; by throwing a metal ball at the window. Rather than bouncing off, it cracked the window significantly. They tried again with the back window, leaving yet another baseball-sized shatter in the glass.<\/p>\n<p>Musk has said he wants the Cybertruck to be bullet-proof. Might we get yet another, potentially louder and more dangerous, demonstration at this week&#8217;s event? When in Texas&#8230;<\/p>\n<h2>Only 10 Cybertrucks delivered<\/h2>\n<p>Tesla&#8217;s global director of product design Javier Verdura said at a keynote address in November that the company intends to deliver 10 Cybertrucks at the event, according to Mexican newspaper <a href=\"https:\/\/www-milenio-com.translate.goog\/negocios\/ven-que-e-autos-superaran-a-los-de-gasolina-para-2026?_x_tr_sl=auto&amp;_x_tr_tl=en&amp;_x_tr_hl=en-US&amp;_x_tr_pto=wapp\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Milenio<\/a>. As Tesla has done before, those first 10 trucks will probably go to Tesla employees and possibly a high-profile individual. For instance, during the Tesla Model X event in 2015 early investors Ira Ehrenpreis and Steve Jurvetson took the stage to take their cars.<\/p>\n<p>Tesla could not be reached to confirm.<\/p>\n<p>The automaker often only delivers a handful of cars during its delivery events. A year ago at <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2022\/12\/01\/tesla-reveals-long-awaited-semi-truck-and-begins-first-deliveries\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Tesla&#8217;s Semi delivery event<\/a>, the automaker handed out about five trucks to Pepsi. And back in 2017, the Model 3&#8217;s initial delivery event saw just 30 cars being delivered, mostly to employees.<\/p>\n<p>Whether 10 is an accurate number, we can also assume a small number of first units to be delivered based on Tesla&#8217;s order agreement that threatened to sue Cybertruck buyers who resell the vehicle without permission during their first year of ownership. <a href=\"https:\/\/techcrunch.com\/2023\/11\/14\/tesla-hits-reverse-on-threat-to-sue-cybertruck-resellers\/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Tesla quickly walked back<\/a> on that language, but automakers usually only include such clauses if they have a limited quantity of vehicles.<\/p>\n<p>While Tesla has clearly set a precedent for anticlimactic delivery events, customers who have been waiting for this day for years may still be disappointed in the modest offering. Musk estimated the Cybertruck has about 1 million reservations during Tesla&#8217;s third-quarter earnings call.<\/p>\n<p>Tesla first announced its Cybertruck in 2019, claiming first deliveries would be scheduled for 2021. The automaker continued to push out the production and delivery dates, due to supply chain headaches and challenges in building the unique vehicle.<\/p>\n","protected":false},"excerpt":{"rendered":"<p>The Tesla Cybertruck, the first of which will be delivered Thursday four years after its debut, is loved and loathed. For fans, it&#8217;s a symbol for what Tesla and its CEO Elon Musk stand for: creativity, irreverence, rebellion. Others see it as an act of hubris. It could be both. Either way, the stakes are [&hellip;]<\/p>\n","protected":false},"author":133574434,"featured_media":2635233,"comment_status":"open","ping_status":"closed","sticky":false,"template":"","format":"standard","meta":{"amp_status":"","relegenceEntities":[],"relegenceSubjects":[],"carmot_uuid":"6f63b7a7-a6b1-3868-8857-5e46248f99ad","footnotes":"","apple_news_api_created_at":"2023-11-29T18:24:35Z","apple_news_api_id":"c88a8ae3-5ab7-41d0-8f15-4075fd20e046","apple_news_api_modified_at":"2023-11-29T18:24:35Z","apple_news_api_revision":"AAAAAAAAAAD\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/w==","apple_news_api_share_url":"http:\/\/web.archive.org\/web\/20231201234640\/https:\/\/apple.news\/AyIqK41q3QdCPFUB1_SDgRg","apple_news_coverimage":0,"apple_news_coverimage_caption":"","apple_news_is_hidden":false,"apple_news_is_paid":false,"apple_news_is_preview":false,"apple_news_is_sponsored":false,"apple_news_maturity_rating":"","apple_news_metadata":"\"\"","apple_news_pullquote":"","apple_news_pullquote_position":"","apple_news_slug":"","apple_news_sections":"\"\"","apple_news_suppress_video_url":false,"apple_news_use_image_component":false},"categories":[2401],"tags":[576716027,1244446,429989],"crunchbase_tag":[],"tc_stories_tax":[],"tc_ec_category":[],"tc_event":[],"tc_regions_tax":[],"yoast_head":"<!-- This site is optimized with the Yoast SEO Premium plugin v20.2.1 (Yoast SEO v20.2.1) - https:\/\/yoast.com\/wordpress\/plugins\/seo\/ -->\n<title>Innovation or folly?
